meta.name: Carne Asada
meta.subtitle: Citrus-garlic marinated steak, grilled (or seared) hot and fast for tacos, bowls, and platters

summary: Classic carne asada with a bright citrus-garlic marinade and hot-and-fast cooking for tender, flavorful slices. SSA baseline includes grill and stovetop methods, plus options for tacos and bowls.

yield.notes: Serves 6 as a main (about 6 oz cooked steak per person).

optional.label: Vacuum Sealer
optional.notes: Great for freezing steak in marinade for future meals

ingredients.label: Flank steak (or skirt steak)
ingredients.notes: Flank is thicker; skirt cooks even faster. Trim excess surface fat/silverskin.

ingredients.label: Fresh cilantro
ingredients.notes: Chopped; omit if you dislike cilantro

ingredients.label: White onion
ingredients.notes: Finely grated or very finely chopped

ingredients.label: Jalapeño
ingredients.notes: Minced; remove seeds for less heat

ingredients.label: Salt
ingredients.notes: Start with 2 tsp; adjust to taste and steak thickness

steps.title: Mix the marinade
steps.body: In a bowl, whisk orange juice, lime juice, olive oil, garlic, and seasonings. If using cilantro, onion, and jalapeño, stir them in. Taste: it should be tangy, salty, and strongly seasoned (it will mellow on the meat).

steps.title: Marinate the steak
steps.body: Place steak in a zip-top bag or shallow dish. Pour in marinade and coat well. Refrigerate 1–4 hours (best balance). For skirt steak (thin), aim for 45–120 minutes. Do not marinate overnight with high citrus unless you prefer a softer texture.

steps.title: Preheat grill or skillet
steps.body: Remove steak from fridge 15–20 minutes before cooking. Preheat grill to high (very hot) or heat a cast-iron skillet over medium-high until smoking lightly. Pat steak lightly—leave some marinade clinging but avoid puddles to reduce steaming.

steps.title: Cook hot and fast
steps.body: Grill or sear steak 3–6 minutes per side depending on thickness. Target internal temp: 125–130°F (52–54°C) for medium-rare, 135°F (57°C) for medium. For skirt steak, watch closely; it cooks quickly.
checks: Internal temp hits target for doneness

temperature.notes: Target 125–130°F for medium-rare; rest will carry over

steps.title: Rest and slice against the grain
steps.body: Rest steak 8–10 minutes. Slice thinly across the grain (important for tenderness). Squeeze lime over slices and garnish with cilantro if desired.

variations.label: No-Grill Stovetop Method
variations.notes: Use a cast-iron skillet or heavy pan. Sear with good ventilation. You can finish in a 425°F oven for thicker cuts if needed.

variations.label: Taco Mode
variations.notes: Chop sliced steak into bite-size pieces. Serve with warm corn tortillas, diced onion, cilantro, salsa, and lime.

variations.label: Bowl Mode
variations.notes: Serve over rice and beans with pico de gallo, avocado, and salsa. Great for meal prep.

variations.label: Milder / Kid-Friendly
variations.notes: Omit jalapeño and chipotle/paprika. Keep citrus + garlic + salt as the main profile.

variations.label: Freezer Pack
variations.notes: Combine steak + marinade in a freezer bag and freeze up to 2 months. Thaw in fridge 24 hours, then cook. This doubles as marinating time.

storage.refrigeration: Store cooked sliced steak 3–4 days. Reheat gently or eat cold in salads/tacos.
storage.freezing: Freeze raw steak in marinade (recommended). Cooked steak can be frozen but may dry out; reheat gently with a splash of broth.
storage.reheat: Warm briefly in a skillet with a splash of water/broth; avoid overcooking.

notes: Discard leftover marinade that touched raw meat (do not reuse without boiling).
Use a thermometer for best doneness and food safety.

prepNotes: Marinade can be mixed 2–3 days ahead.
Slice across the grain for tenderness—especially for flank/skirt.
planner.leftoversPlan: Use leftovers in breakfast tacos, salads, burrito bowls, or chilaquiles topping.

notes: Citrus marinades work quickly; avoid overly long marination on thin cuts to prevent mushy texture.
For best char, keep heat high and surface relatively dry (pat off excess marinade).
If you prefer a more 'street-style' profile, add a pinch of oregano and a splash of beer to the marinade.
